There are interwoven tensions running through all negotiations. We've gone over a few of them so far, levels of trust, layers of emotions, and visions of leverage. In this course, I want to introduce another hidden yet ruthless tension. It's rarely accounted for and may kill up to 50% of all deals. The tension behind the table on the other side, because there's always a team on the other side. Whether it be a spouse whispering in their ear, the in-house attorney who wishes they were the one at the deal table and can't wait to poke holes in the agreement after it's signed, or the person who's affected by the deal once it's signed and resents not being asked in advance. There are always conflicting tensions among those behind the table on the other side. And because of that, you have to gain the upper hand in the negotiation while simultaneously giving your counterpart the illusion of control. Why? Do you want them feeling out of control? How are you going to affect those critical dynamics through your counterpart if your counterpart feels either out of control or cut out entirely? You can't! If your counterpart feels out of control, your efforts to extend your influence to the rest of his or her team will evaporate. This contradicts the myth about getting to the decision maker to protect your deal, as if that person is all powerful. It's not the decision maker you have to worry about. It's the deal killer. We in the FBI came to find this out the hard way that if you didn't account for the deal killers, your deal would get killed. It always takes longer to do something over that it does to do it right the first time. And many times, you don't get do-overs. How do you begin to gain the upper hand so you can influence the team on the other side? Let me ask you this. Who most likely has the upper hand in a conversation? The person listening or the person talking?
